name: Real-World Capture Architecture
consequences: Immediate: Tiered sensing reduces on-site workload by 30% while maintaining safety. → Systemic: 25% faster scaling via standardized pipelines and QC. → Strategic: Enables rapid multi-site expansion; trade-off: privacy controls and data resolution.
options: Maintain a tightly controlled in-house capture architecture with fixed nightly PSG on a static schedule and dedicated night staff to maximize data fidelity and safety.
Adopt a tiered sensing approach with nightly low-burden monitoring supplemented by periodic enhanced PSG on predefined nights, preserving data quality while reducing on-site burden.

Install modular portable pods with privacy-preserving edge analytics and a data-licensing framework that allows external researchers access to de-identified, synchronized data via federated processing.
review: Controls Speed of deployment vs Safety and data fidelity. Weakness: The options fail to consider regulatory compliance delays and staff fatigue that accumulate with rapid ramp-up.

name: Funding and Governance Flexibility
consequences: Immediate: Move to milestone-driven funding with flexible governance authority to reallocate resources mid-course. → Systemic: 25% faster scaling via dynamic staffing and procurement adjustments, reducing overhead by 15%. → Strategic: Improves resilience against funding swings and accelerates outcomes, but increases governance complexity and potential misalignment among partners.
options: Maintain traditional grant-funded model with annual budgets and centralized governance, prioritizing risk minimization and regulatory compliance.
Institute milestone-based funding with a light external advisory consortium to guide prioritization, enabling faster pivots without sacrificing accountability.
Create a cross-institution data-licensing cooperative with a venture-like fund and tiered access to de-identified datasets, supported by private contributions to accelerate expansion.
review: Controls Budget rigidity vs Strategic agility. Weakness: The options fail to consider cross-institution governance complexity and potential delays in milestone validation.

name: Data Stewardship and Privacy Model
consequences: Immediate: Enforce strict de-identification and access controls, reducing privacy risk by 60%. → Systemic: 25% faster scaling via privacy-preserving APIs and secure data fabrics. → Strategic: Enables broader collaborations with industry and academia while maintaining regulatory compliance; Trade-off: data richness may decline slightly and require consent management resources.
options: Maintain local, non-shared datasets with on-site scoring and no external data exports to minimize risk.
Establish de-identification pipelines and a controlled-access data portal for approved researchers under data-use agreements.
Adopt privacy-preserving on-device analytics with federated processing across sites and a privacy-by-design data commons managing dynamic consent.
review: Controls Privacy vs Data utility. Weakness: The options fail to consider consent fatigue and the risk of re-identification in shared datasets.

name: Recruitment and Cohort Integration
consequences: Immediate: Pre-screening and centralized consent accelerate enrollment. → Systemic: 15% faster enrollment and 20% reduced screen-outs via standardized pre-screening. → Strategic: Timely enrollment supports milestone achievement and cohort diversity; trade-off: increased governance overhead and privacy concerns with broader networks.
options: Rely on institutional referral channels and strict pre-screening within Bonn to ensure study integrity.
Broaden recruitment via regional neurologists and patient advocacy networks, with standardized screening and outreach.
Establish a cross-region recruitment consortium with a cloud-based eligibility engine and a pay-for-performance outreach model under a centralized consent framework.
review: Controls Recruitment velocity vs Cohort representativeness. Weakness: The options fail to consider potential bias from centralized recruitment and network population differences.

name: Automation and Sensing Cascade
consequences: Immediate: Rule-based event triage reduces manual scoring time by 40% and improves inter-rater reliability. → Systemic: 25% faster data annotation workflow due to standardized validation layers and real-time QC dashboards. → Strategic: Cuts marginal cost per participant and accelerates dissemination; trade-off: automated triage may miss atypical events without sufficient human oversight.
options: Maintain the current sensing stack with manual scoring by two independent raters and incremental hardware upgrades only when necessary.
Introduce rule-based event triage pipelines to reduce manual review time while maintaining human oversight.
Deploy end-to-end automated triage with privacy-preserving edge analytics and cross-site knowledge sharing through a neutral data commons to accelerate iteration.
review: Controls Automation productivity vs Human oversight. Weakness: The options fail to consider hidden costs of maintenance and regulatory compliance for automated systems.
